---
title: "Master's Thesis Seminar"
subtitle: "Seismic Hazard Mapping In Japan: effects on land and real estate prices"
author: "Anthony Rocher -- ENS Lyon"
format: revealjs
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: false
bibliography: references.bib
---

## Research Question & Motivation

::: {.callout-note title="Research Question"}
[How costly it is to be exposed to natural disasters?]{style="color: #C0392B;"}
:::

**Why does it matter?**

Intensifying disasters with climate change.

Disasters: floods, hurricanes, wildfires, and droughts.

In Japan: earthquakes, tsunamis.

**The setting**: Hazard risk mapping in Japan and its consequences on real estate prices.

## Literature review

Many papers on natural disasters and their consequences on housing prices (@brookshire_test_1985, @hidano_effect_2015, @balboni_harms_2025 among many others).

My work heavily relies on @ikefuji2022.

**My main contributions:**

-   What about the unaffected areas but still at risk?

-   Extending the analysis to the housing market

-   Controling for other type of risk (not done by the authors)

## Data (1)

::::::: columns
:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Risk mapping data

-   **Japan Meteorological Agency Records (JMA)**
-   all earthquakes records
-   coordinates / magnitude
-   **Japan Seismic Hazard Information Station (J-SHIS)**
-   risk hazard maps
-   change over time
:::
::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Real estate data

-   **Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (MLIT)**
-   accurate description of the properties (nearest station, type of use, etc.)
-   **LIFULL HOMES**
-   including rentals
-   two limitations: estimation only,, from 2015 to 2016 only
:::
::::
:::::::

## Data (2)

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](images/shm_2020_style.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](images/shm_2016_final.png)
:::
:::::

## Data (3)

Ma base de données actuelle :

::: callout-tip
LIFFULL HOMES + CARTES DE RISQUE + VARIABLES AGRÉGÉES
:::

-   Les séismes restent à intégrer

-   Si j'ai le temps, j'intégrerai aussi les données du MLIT (land prices)

## Empirical Strategy (1)

**Groupe test** :

-   les bâtiments qui se situent dans les zones sismiques dont la probabilité d'occurence au cours des 30 prochaines années est supérieure à 0.26.

**Groupe de contrôle** :

-   les bâtiments situés dans les zones inférieures à ce seuil
-   peut-être ceux qui sont situés dans les zones plus sûres

## Empirical Strategy (2)

:::: {.callout-warning title="In progress"}
::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; text-align: center;"}
$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln P_{it} = \alpha_0 &+ \sum_{j=1}^{J} \alpha_j Z_{ij} + \beta r_i + \gamma Seismic_i + \delta Earthquake_{it} \\
&+ \theta_t (Earthquake_{it} \times Seismic_i) + \varepsilon_{it}
\end{aligned}
$$
:::
::::

::: {style="font-size: 0.6em; line-height: 1.2;"}
-   $Z_{ij}$: vector of property and location controls
-   $r_i$: location fixed effects (prefectures)
-   $Seismic_i$: baseline seismic risk exposure (over $p = 0.3$)
-   $Earthquake_{it}$: earthquake occurrence/intensity at time $t$
-   $Earthquake_{it} \times Seismic_i$: interaction capturing heterogeneous effects

$Z_{ij}$ includes aggregated data such as population, net migration, employment rate, facilities (e.g. number of hospitals, shcools, a municipality financial index), and some macro variables (GDP, business index)
:::

## References
